title: Skordalia
subtitle: Greek garlic and potato dip
excerpt: A thick Greek dip of mashed potato and raw garlic, beaten with olive oil and lemon until smooth and pungent. Served alongside fried fish, grilled vegetables, or beetroot salad.
sourceNotes: Skordalia is one of the oldest Greek condiments, appearing in cookery records across the Mediterranean. Documented in Claudia Roden, A New Book of Middle Eastern Food (1985) and Vefa Alexiadou, Greek Cuisine (1991). The potato version is the most common in modern Greece; an older version uses bread or almonds as the base.

recipe.yieldDescription: Serves 4 as a dip or sauce
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps covered in the fridge for up to 2 days. Bring to room temperature before serving; thin with a little water if it thickens.

content.text: Skordalia is Greek in spirit and aggressive in flavour. The garlic is raw and plentiful; the olive oil is beaten in generously; the result is a sharp, pale dip that clears the sinuses and demands bread. It is not a subtle thing. Served alongside battered fried fish or roasted beetroot, it is exactly right.

The potato must be floury and freshly cooked — waxy potatoes turn gluey when beaten and the texture suffers. Mash thoroughly while still hot, then beat in the garlic and oil while it is still warm so the oil emulsifies into the potato rather than pooling.

items.prepNote: floury variety such as Maris Piper, peeled and cut into large chunks
crushed to a paste with a little salt

content.text: Boil the potato in well-salted water until completely tender, about 20 minutes. Drain thoroughly and let the steam escape for a minute or two. Mash while hot and very dry — any residual moisture will make the finished dip watery.
Beat in the garlic and oil

Crush the garlic to a fine paste with the flat of a knife and a pinch of salt. Add to the warm potato and beat together with a wooden spoon. Add the olive oil in a thin stream, beating constantly, alternating with splashes of warm water and the lemon juice. The dip should be smooth, creamy, and just loose enough to spoon. Season with salt and add the red wine vinegar if you want more sharpness.

items.cause: Waxy potato variety used, or potatoes beaten too vigorously in a food processor
items.fix: Use floury potatoes (Maris Piper) and work by hand with a masher; never use a food processor for skordalia

items.cause: Oil added too fast or potato was cold when the oil was beaten in
items.fix: Work with warm potato; add the oil slowly, beating well between each addition

items.cause: Six cloves of raw garlic is the traditional amount — the flavour is assertive by design
items.fix: Reduce to 3 to 4 cloves for a milder result; alternatively, roast the garlic first for a sweeter, less sharp flavour

items.cause: Potatoes were very starchy or not enough liquid was added
items.fix: Beat in warm water a tablespoon at a time until the consistency loosens

content.text: Skordalia (from skordo, garlic) is eaten wherever fried or grilled fish is on the table, and alongside salt-cod fritters (bakaliaros) on 25th March for Greek Independence Day. The bread-based version predates the potato version by centuries and is still made in certain regions. The dish has close relatives across the Mediterranean: Spanish alioli, Provençal aïoli, and Italian agliata all share the same garlic-fat principle.
